title: GitLab CI/CD - Hands-On Lab: Configuring Pipeline Testing

Task B. Create test stage and job in .gitlab-ci.yml
text:
GitLab CI/CD - Hands-On Lab: Configuring Pipeline Testing

Estimated time to complete: 15 minutes

Estimated time to complete: 15 minutes

With automated builds and controlled Merge Request pipelines in place, your team faced a critical realization: they were building and deploying code without any automated quality checks. The problems became apparent quickly:

Bugs Reaching Production : Code that compiles successfully still contains logical errors that break functionality

No Test Automation : Developers run tests manually on their local machines, leading to inconsistent coverage

Blocking Pipeline Failures : When experimental tests fail, they halt the entire pipeline, preventing releases even when core functionality works

Zero Visibility on Code Quality : Management has no metrics on test coverage or code health

Late Bug Discovery : Issues are discovered by users in production rather than caught early in development

In this lab, you’ll implement automated testing in your CI/CD pipeline, including unit tests for your Go application. You’ll learn to organize different test types, handle test failures gracefully using the allow_failure keyword, and establish the foundation for comprehensive quality gates that catch bugs before they reach production.

Task A. Create code and tests

Let’s introduce some code to test, as well as some unit tests for the code.

Navigate to your project.

Select + > New directory .

Select + > New directory .

Set the directory name to ArrayUtils .

Set the directory name to ArrayUtils .

Task A. Create code and tests

Let’s introduce some code to test, as well as some unit tests for the code.

Navigate to your project.

Navigate to your project.

Select + > New directory .

Select + > New directory .

Set the directory name to ArrayUtils .

Set the directory name to ArrayUtils .

Make sure that Commit to the current main branch is selected, and click Commit changes .

Make sure that Commit to the current main branch is selected, and click Commit changes .

In the ArrayUtils directory, select + > New file .

In the ArrayUtils directory, select + > New file .

Name the file ArrayUtils.go . Add the following code to the file: package ArrayUtils func SearchArray ( a [] int , x int ) bool { var found bool found = false for i := 0 ; i < len ( a ); i ++ { if a [ i ] == x { found = true } } return found }

Name the file ArrayUtils.go . Add the following code to the file:

Select Commit changes , add an appropriate commit message, and select Commit changes .

Select Commit changes , add an appropriate commit message, and select Commit changes .

In your ArrayUtils directory, select + > New file .

In your ArrayUtils directory, select + > New file .

Name the file ArrayUtils_test.go . In this file, we will add our unit tests: package ArrayUtils import ( "testing" ) func TestArraySearch ( t * testing . T ){ var a = [] int { 1 , 2 , 3 } var result bool result = SearchArray ( a , 3 ) if ! result { t . Fatalf ( "Expected to find value!" ); } result = SearchArray ( a , 5 ) if result { t . Fatalf ( "Expected to not find value!" ); } }

Task B. Create test stage and job in .gitlab-ci.yml

Generally, tests will run inside of the test stage of a CI/CD process.

Select Build > Pipeline Editor .

Select Build > Pipeline Editor .

In the stages section, add the test stage before the build stage: stages : - test - build - run - release

In the stages section, add the test stage before the build stage:

Create a job in the test stage that runs the tests we created for ArrayUtils. test go : stage : test script : go test array/ArrayUtils

Create a job in the test stage that runs the tests we created for ArrayUtils.

After adding these changes, select Commit changes .

After adding these changes, select Commit changes .

Create a job in the test stage that runs the tests we created for ArrayUtils.

After adding these changes, select Commit changes .

After adding these changes, select Commit changes .

View the resulting pipeline. You will now see a test stage appear. In the test stage, you will see your test job. When you select the test job, you will see that the job runs the go test array/ArrayUtils command. If all of your code is correct, this stage should succeed without any errors. Note that when we add the test stage to the start of our stages block, it precedes our other stages such as release. In this setup, if the tests fail for any reason, the stages afterwards will not run. In some cases, we may still want future stages to run if our current job fails. The next task will show you how to do that.

Task C. Creating a failable job

To allow a job to fail, you can add the allow_failure attribute to a job. Add the allow_failure attribute to your test job so it looks like the code below: test go : stage : test script : go test array/ArrayUtils allow_failure : true

To allow a job to fail, you can add the allow_failure attribute to a job. Add the allow_failure attribute to your test job so it looks like the code below:

To test this out, try adding a new test that will always fail. If you are unsure on how to write the test, edit your ArrayUtils_test.go file and copy the code below. package ArrayUtils import ( "testing" ) func TestArraySearch ( t * testing . T ){ var a = [] int { 1 , 2 , 3 } var result bool result = SearchArray ( a , 3 ) if ! result { t . Fatalf ( "Expected to find value!" ); } result = SearchArray ( a , 5 ) if result { t . Fatalf ( "Expected to not find value!" ); } result = SearchArray ( a , 7 ) if ! result { t . Fatalf ( "Expected to find value!" ); } } In this example, the final test looks for a value that does not exist in the array, but expects it to find the value. This test will always fail as we are expecting the wrong result.

Select Commit changes and commit the changes to a new branch called failable-tests-branch . Commit the code without a Merge Request.

Select Commit changes and commit the changes to a new branch called failable-tests-branch . Commit the code without a Merge Request.

Monitor the progress of your test job. When the test job completes, you will see that the job fails. When it fails, it will show a yellow exclamation mark rather than a red x. This indicates that the job failed as a warning, meaning it won’t prevent future stages from running. You will see that your next stage does execute even with the failure.

Monitor the progress of your test job.

With you implementing automated testing, your team shifted from “hope it works” to “know it works” deployments. Unit tests now run automatically on every code commit, catching logical errors and edge cases before code even reaches the build stage. The test stage executes first in the pipeline, ensuring that broken code never wastes resources on unnecessary builds or releases. When the team introduced the allow_failure attribute for experimental or non-critical tests, they gained flexibility—allowing pipelines to complete even when optional tests fail, while still maintaining visibility into potential issues through the warning indicators.

You have completed this lab exercise. You can view the other lab guides for this course .

If you wish to make a change to the lab, please submit your changes via Merge Request.
